summary: Andrew Carnegie argues that while the concentration of wealth in the hands of a few is an inevitable and beneficial result of industrial capitalism, the wealthy have a moral obligation to redistribute their surplus. He outlines three ways to dispose of wealth, concluding that administering it during one's lifetime for the public good is the only noble path. He strongly warns against leaving fortunes to heirs or giving indiscriminate charity, advocating instead for funding institutions that help people help themselves.

principles.statement: Accept and welcome great inequality of environment and the concentration of business as essential for the future progress of the race.
principles.rationale: The law of competition and concentration of capital brings cheaper, better commodities and ensures the survival of the fittest, elevating the entire race even if it is hard on individuals.
principles.source_quote: We accept and welcome therefore, as conditions to which we must accommodate ourselves, great inequality of environment, the concentration of business, industrial and commercial, in the hands of a few, and the law of competition between these, as being not only beneficial, but essential for the future progress of the race.

principles.statement: Consider all surplus revenues simply as trust funds to be administered for the community.
principles.rationale: The wealthy possess superior talent for organization and management, making them the best agents to direct surplus capital for the public good, doing better for the poor than they would or could do for themselves.
principles.source_quote: ...to consider all surplus revenues which come to him simply as trust funds, which he is called upon to administer, and strictly bound as a matter of duty to administer in the manner which, in his judgment, is best calculated to produce the most beneficial results for the community...

principles.statement: In bestowing charity, the main consideration should be to help those who will help themselves.
principles.rationale: Alms-giving degrades the individual and the race. True philanthropy provides the means for aspiring people to improve themselves, rather than rewarding vice or sloth.
principles.source_quote: In bestowing charity, the main consideration should be to help those who will help themselves; to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so; to give those who desire to use the aids by which they may rise; to assist, but rarely or never to do all.

frameworks.name: The Duty of the Man of Wealth
frameworks.when_to_apply: When a person of great wealth is determining how to live and dispose of their accumulated fortune.
steps: Set an example of modest, unostentatious living, shunning display or extravagance.
Provide moderately for the legitimate wants of those dependent upon him.
Administer all surplus revenues as trust funds for the community to produce the most beneficial results.
frameworks.source_quote: This, then, is held to be the duty of the man of Wealth: First, to set an example of modest, unostentatious living, shunning display or extravagance; to provide moderately for the legitimate wants of those dependent upon him; and after doing so to consider all surplus revenues which come to him simply as trust funds...

mental_models.name: The Millionaire as Trustee
mental_models.description: Viewing the wealthy capitalist not as the ultimate owner of their surplus wealth, but as a temporary steward or agent entrusted by society to manage and distribute it for the common good.
mental_models.example: Instead of distributing profits as higher wages in small, easily wasted amounts, the trustee pools the wealth to build a lasting public institution like the Cooper Institute or a free library.

mental_models.name: Ladders for the Aspiring
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating philanthropic investments. Instead of giving direct handouts, wealth should be used to build infrastructure that enables ambitious individuals to climb out of poverty.
mental_models.example: Funding parks, works of art, and public institutions like libraries that people must actively use to benefit from.

heuristics: Of every thousand dollars spent in so called charity to-day, it is probable that $950 is unwisely spent.
I would as soon leave to my son a curse as the almighty dollar.
The man who dies thus rich dies disgraced.

signature_quotes.text: The problem of our age is the proper administration of wealth, so that the ties of brotherhood may still bind together the rich and poor in harmonious relationship.
signature_quotes.context: Opening sentence of the essay, establishing the core thesis.

signature_quotes.text: The man who dies thus rich dies disgraced.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding thought on those who hoard their wealth until death rather than administering it during their lives.

signature_quotes.text: Individualism will continue, but the millionaire will be but a trustee for the poor; intrusted for a season with a great part of the increased wealth of the community, but administering it for the community far better than it could or would have done for itself.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the ideal state where capitalism and philanthropy merge.

signature_quotes.text: It were better for mankind that the millions of the rich were thrown in to the sea than so spent as to encourage the slothful, the drunken, the unworthy.
signature_quotes.context: Warning against the dangers of indiscriminate charity.

anti_patterns.description: Leaving massive fortunes to children or heirs.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It burdens the children, ruins their work ethic, and is usually driven by misguided family pride rather than the actual welfare of the child or the state.

anti_patterns.description: Bequeathing wealth for public uses only at death.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It shows the person would have kept the money if they could take it with them. The testator's wishes are often thwarted, and it requires just as much ability to spend wealth wisely as it does to make it.
It rewards vice rather than relieving virtue, encouraging the slothful and drunken while doing more injury than good.

anti_patterns.description: Attempting to overturn Individualism and Private Property (Communism/Socialism).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It attacks the foundation of civilization itself, requiring a fundamental change in human nature that is not practicable, rather than working within the highest results of human experience.
